answer:
Across the Sanskrit smriti tradition, 'dharma' carries a cluster of interlocking meanings — scriptural injunction, cosmic/moral order, sacred duty, and right conduct — each text emphasising a different facet.

1. Āṅgirasa Smṛti (unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1, ch. 1): The text opens with the most philosophically precise definition in the corpus. Dharma is equated with codanā — a scriptural injunction carried by specific Vedic grammatical markers (optative Liṅ, imperative Loṭ, gerundive Tavya). 'Dharma is said to be that which is a scriptural injunction (codanā); anything else is so only by extension. That codanā is to be known by its indicators and other such signs; when enjoined by the Śruti, it grants liberation.' (Ang_1.3). Only injunctions carried by the śruti (revealed Veda) count fully; Purāṇic or smṛti-derived injunctions are derivative. This is a Mīmāṃsā-aligned position: dharma is fundamentally a deontological command embedded in revealed scripture.

2. Manusmṛti (unknown-manusmrti-347b76, chs. 1–2): Manu treats dharma on two registers. Cosmologically, dharma is one of the paired opposites built into creation: 'in order to distinguish actions, he separated Dharma from non-Dharma, and he yoked these creatures with the pairs of opposites, such as pleasure and pain' (Mn_1.26). Dharma is also age-relative and varna-relative: 'In the Krita age, Dharma is four-footed and complete… Dharma diminishes by one foot in each [subsequent age]' (Mn_1.81–82), and 'The Dharmas of men are different in the Krita age… in proportion to the decline of each age' (Mn_1.85). The highest dharma in each age shifts: tapas → knowledge → sacrifice → giving (Mn_1.86). Epistemically, chapter 2 enumerates the fourfold sources of dharma: 'The entire Veda is the root-source of Dharma, as are Smṛti and the conduct of those who know it, the practices of the virtuous, and one's own self-satisfaction' (Mn_2.6). Elsewhere: 'The Veda, the sacred tradition, the conduct of the virtuous, and what is pleasing to one's own soul — these they declare to be the fourfold, direct evidence of Dharma' (Mn_2.12). Manu also supplies a sociological definition: 'That which is practiced by the learned and the good, who are ever free from hatred and passion, and which is assented to by the heart — know that to be Dharma' (Mn_2.1).

3. Yājñavalkya Smṛti (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6, ch. 1): Yājñavalkya synthesises and expands. The sources of dharma are listed comprehensively: 'Revelation (the Vedas), tradition (the Smṛtis), the conduct of the virtuous, what is pleasing to one's own self, and desire born of right resolve — this is declared to be the root of dharma' (Yj_1.7). A further definition is functional: 'Whatever gift, accompanied by faith, is given to a worthy recipient at the right place and time, and by the proper means — all that is the mark of dharma' (Yj_1.6). He also identifies a hierarchy: 'Of sacrifice, right conduct, sense-control, non-violence, giving, and the study of the Veda — of these actions, this is the highest dharma: the vision of the Self through yoga' (Yj_1.8). Additionally, the Purāṇas, Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Dharmaśāstra, and the Aṅgas together with the Vedas are called 'the fourteen sources of knowledge and of dharma' (Yj_1.3).

4. Parāśara Smṛti (unknown-parasara-smrti-2259be, ch. 1): Parāśara emphasises temporal relativism. Dharma is yuga-specific: different texts govern different cosmic ages — Manu's dharmas for the Kṛta yuga, Gautama's for the Tretā, Śaṅkha-Likhita's for the Dvāpara, and Parāśara's own for the Kali yuga (Par_1.24). The highest virtue shifts accordingly: tapas (Kṛta), knowledge (Tretā), sacrifice (Dvāpara), charity (Kali) (Par_1.23). Proper conduct (ācāra) is identified as dharma's guardian: 'Ācāra (proper conduct) is the protector of dharma for all four varṇas. For those whose bodies are corrupted by bad conduct, dharma turns away' (Par_1.37).

Synthesis: Across these smriti texts, dharma operates on four levels simultaneously: (a) ontological — it is the moral/cosmic order built into creation and into the structure of time's ages; (b) scriptural-deontological — it is what the Veda enjoins through grammatical injunction (codanā), with smṛti and the conduct of the virtuous as secondary sources; (c) social-functional — it is varna-specific and ashrama-specific duty, defining the proper role and actions of each class and life-stage; and (d) soteriological — it leads to liberation when followed, and its highest form is the vision of the Self through yoga (Yājñavalkya) or Vedic karma leading to liberation (Āṅgirasa). The texts converge on Veda as the root-source, smṛti as its remembered extension, and the conduct of the virtuous (sadācāra) as its living embodiment.

tool_calls.name: list_works
tool_calls.result_summary: Found 11 Sanskrit works including 8 smriti texts: Āṅgirasa, Bṛhaspati, Kātyāyana, Manusmṛti, Nāradasmṛti, Parāśara, Viṣṇu, Vīramitrodaya, Yama, Yājñavalkya Smṛtis

tool_calls.name: search_corpus
tool_calls.result_summary: 30 hits, all from Āṅgirasa Smṛti ch. 1, including the definitional passage Ang_1.3 equating dharma with codanā

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Confirmed Ang_1.3 definition of dharma as codanā, Ang_1.4 restricting it to Śruti grammatical markers

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Obtained Mn_1.26 (dharma separated from adharma at creation), Mn_1.81–86 (four-footed dharma diminishing across yugas, age-specific highest dharma), Mn_1.98–99 (Brahmana as embodiment of dharma)

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Obtained Mn_2.1 (dharma as what the learned and good practice), Mn_2.6 (Veda + smṛti + conduct of virtuous + self-satisfaction as root sources), Mn_2.10 (Śruti and Smṛti as non-questionable sources), Mn_2.12 (fourfold evidence of dharma)

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Obtained Yj_1.3 (fourteen sources of dharma), Yj_1.6 (gift at right place/time/means as mark of dharma), Yj_1.7 (Veda+smṛti+conduct+self+right resolve as root of dharma), Yj_1.8 (highest dharma = vision of Self through yoga)

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Obtained Par_1.2 (dharma = righteous duty and cosmic law beneficial for mankind), Par_1.16 (dharmas differ by yuga), Par_1.22–24 (yuga-specific dharmas and texts)

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Obtained Par_1.23 (tapas/knowledge/sacrifice/charity supreme in successive yugas), Par_1.24 (Manu for Kṛta, Parāśara for Kali), Par_1.37 (ācāra as protector of dharma)

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Viṣṇu Smṛti ch.1 is cosmogonic narrative (Boar incarnation); dharma appears as attribute of the divine form (Vi_1.5) but no standalone definition in these opening verses

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Yama Smṛti ch.1 focuses on penances and purification; no explicit definition of dharma in opening passages
